description: Figure 1 presents the room-temperature microhardness HU (in N/mm²) of (111)-oriented Fe-16%Al monocrystals as a function of various prior heat treatments. The x-axis lists conditions including as-grown, furnace cooling (FC) from 1100 °C to room temperature or to 600 °C with subsequent water quench, annealing at 250–600 °C for various durations (e.g., 250 °C/14 d, 300 °C/6 h to 14 d, 320 °C/223 d, 400 °C/6 h, 600 °C/6 h), direct water quench from 1100 °C (Q*), and Q* followed by 300 °C/14 d anneal. Each data point shows the mean HU value with error bars. The hardness varies by at most ~10% across all treatments, indicating insensitivity to heat treatment. Notably, the quenched specimen (Q*) exhibits the highest hardness (~2100 N/mm²), attributed to excess vacancies trapped by quenching. Intermediate hardness values are observed for furnace-cooled and short-term annealed samples (~1975–2005 N/mm²), while longer anneals at 300 °C and higher temperatures (320–400 °C) yield slightly lower values (~1875–1900 N/mm²). The data support the conclusion that short-range atomic order has no major impact on hardness, whereas quenched-in excess vacancies contribute significantly to hardening, with slow healing-out kinetics during subsequent annealing.

Figure 2 presents room-temperature compressive stress-strain curves for [112]-oriented Fe-16%Al monocrystals tested at a compression rate of 10^-4 s^-1, comparing four prior heat-treatment conditions: as-grown, 1100 °C furnace cooling (FC), 1100 °C FC down to 600 °C with subsequent water quench (600 °C Q), and water quench from 1100 °C followed by annealing at 300 °C for 14 days (1100 °C Q + 300 °C/14d). The curves for the as-grown, FC, and FC+600 °C Q specimens cluster closely, showing similar yield stresses around 420–450 MPa and comparable work-hardening behavior up to 5–6% strain. In contrast, the 1100 °C Q + 300 °C/14d curve displays a distinctly higher yield stress (~460 MPa) and stronger work hardening, attaining a flow stress near 500 MPa at larger strains. This comparison indicates that interrupting furnace cooling at 600 °C does not markedly alter the mechanical response, whereas quenching from 1100 °C—which introduces excess vacancies—significantly increases strength. The figure supports the core conclusion that only heat treatments at temperatures above 600 °C substantially affect the deformation behavior of Fe-Al alloys.

Figure 3 presents the compressive yield stress versus aluminum content for polycrystalline as-cast Fe-Al alloys tested at room temperature. Open circles denote a compression rate of 10^-2 s^-1 and filled circles denote 10^-4 s^-1. Both data sets show a nearly linear increase of yield stress with Al content, rising from approximately 50 MPa for pure Fe to about 470 MPa for Fe-18Al, with fitted lines indicating strengthening rates of 21 and 23 MPa per at.% Al for the higher and lower rates respectively. The Fe-18%Al alloy is marked by a triangle symbol, and the caption states that this alloy yielded with concurrent deformation twinning at both compression rates. The plot demonstrates that the linear yield-stress increase across the 0–18 at.% range is attributed to solid-solution hardening described by Suzuki's theory for bcc alloys, with an overall hardening coefficient of about 2.2 GPa, and the small separation between the two strain rates indicates weak rate sensitivity.

Figure 4 assembles tensile stress-strain curves obtained at a tension rate of 10^-4 s^-1 for polycrystalline as-cast Fe-Al alloys. Panel (a) at room temperature (RT) displays curves for Fe-4at%Al, Fe-11at%Al, Fe-14at%Al, Fe-16at%Al, and Fe-18at%Al, while panel (b) at -100°C includes an additional Fe-17at%Al curve together with the remaining compositions. Both panels show that increasing Al content monotonically raises the yield and flow stresses while reducing the fracture strain. At RT, the Fe-18at%Al curve exhibits visible coarse serrations. At -100°C, the Fe-4at%Al alloy shows a pronounced yield stress drop, and the higher-Al alloys fracture at progressively lower strains accompanied by large load drops. The caption and accompanying text state that the coarse serrations reflect deformation twinning, that RT fracture is ductile, and that fracture at -100°C becomes increasingly brittle with rising Al content.

Figure 5 presents two line-graph panels comparing the tensile response of polycrystalline as-cast Fe–Al alloys (4–18 at% Al) tested at 10^−4 s^−1 at room temperature (a) and −100 °C (b). The left y-axis records strength in MPa and the right y-axis records strain in %. As stated in the caption, the data series represent tensile yield stress, ultimate tensile strength, strain before necking, and fracture strain. The filled circles (●) exhibit a monotonic, nearly linear rise from approximately 140 MPa at 4% Al to about 440 MPa at 18% Al at room temperature, matching the reported yield-stress increase attributed to solid-solution hardening; at −100 °C the same trend is shifted upward by roughly 80 MPa. The filled inverted triangles (▼) trace higher strength values that decline with increasing Al content. The open symbols correspond to strain metrics read against the right-hand scale; the caption reports that both strain before necking and fracture strain decrease with increasing Al content, dropping steeply above 15% Al at room temperature and above 12% Al at −100 °C where they converge, signaling a transition to brittle fracture without necking. Panel (b) additionally plots filled triangles (▲) that the caption identifies as the stress to initiate deformation twinning at −100 °C, appearing for Al contents above approximately 14%. Error bars denote experimental scatter across averaged tests.

Figure 6 presents two transmission-electron micrographs (a, b) illustrating dislocation distributions in polycrystalline Fe-12%Al (with 60 ppm C) after 5.5% compressive straining at 10^-4 s^-1 at room temperature. Both images share a scale bar of 0.4 μm. Subfigure (a) shows the microstructure of the sample quenched from 1100 °C, exhibiting glide bands with dislocation tangles between them. Subfigure (b) shows the sample furnace-cooled from 1100 °C, where deformation initiated dislocation cell formation. The comparison demonstrates that thermal history strongly influences the deformation microstructure: quenching produces tangled dislocations between glide bands, whereas furnace cooling leads to cellular dislocation structures. As the accompanying text states, before deformation the quenched alloy contained only few straight dislocations, while the furnace-cooled alloy showed nearly none. This figure supports the conclusion that the prior heat treatment condition governs the dislocation evolution and work-hardening response during room-temperature deformation in iron-rich iron-aluminum alloys.

Figure 7 presents two transmission-electron micrographs showing dislocation distributions in polycrystalline Fe-16%Al (with less than 20 ppm C) after 5.5% compressive straining at room temperature and a strain rate of 10^-4 s^-1. Both subfigures share a 0.2 μm scale bar. Subfigure (a) shows the microstructure of the quenched sample (from 1100 °C), revealing glide bands oriented in various directions with primarily straight dislocations between them. Subfigure (b) shows the furnace-cooled sample (from 1100 °C), where deformation produced dislocation tangles between the glide bands. The pronounced contrast between the straight dislocations in the quenched condition and the tangled dislocations in the furnace-cooled condition demonstrates a strong effect of thermal history on the deformation substructure. This figure supports the conclusion that quenching suppresses dislocation multiplication and tangling in favor of planar slip, whereas furnace cooling promotes dislocation interactions and tangle formation during plastic deformation of Fe-16%Al.
